verbatim: [§10] For he has made plain to us through all the Prophets that he needs neither sacrifices nor burnt-offerings nor oblations, saying in one place, What is the multitude of your sacrifices unto me? saith the Lord. I am full of burnt offerings and desire not the fat of lambs and the blood of bulls and goats, not even when ye come to appear before me. ... [§16] These things then he abolished in order that the new law of our Lord Jesus Christ, which is without the yoke of necessity, might have its oblation not made by man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes a contemporary 'new law' for his group that explicitly abolishes the requirement for material sacrifices and burnt offerings found in the old law.

verbatim: [§28] And Moses understood and cast the two tables out of his hands, and their covenant was broken, in order that the covenant of Jesus the Beloved should be sealed in our hearts in hope of his faith.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author argues that the 'other' group (the Jews, referred to as 'they'/Israel) lost their right to the covenant, which was instead redirected to the author's own group ('ours').

verbatim: [§21] But to us he says, Behold this is the fast which I chose, saith the Lord, loose every bond of wickedness, set loose the fastenings of harsh agreements, send away the bruised in forgiveness, and tear up every unjust contract, give to the hungry thy bread, and if thou seest a naked man clothe him, bring the homeless into thy house.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a specific set of active social and legal behaviors as the mandatory 'fast' for the Christian group, contrasting it with the physical fasting of the out-group.

verbatim: [§30] [2] Let us give no freedom to our souls to have power to walk with sinners and wicked men, lest we be made like to them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author establishes a behavioral rule for his followers (the in-group) that explicitly restricts their freedom to associate with those categorized as 'sinners and wicked men'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§35] [7] It is ours: but in this way did they finally lose it when Moses had just received it... [§36] [8] But they turned to idols and lost it. For thus saith the Lord: Moses... thy people... have broken the Law. And Moses understood and cast the two tables out of his hands, and their covenant was broken.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author contrasts his own group with Israel, arguing that the Jews lost their access to the divine covenant specifically because they turned to idols at Sinai, resulting in the breaking of the physical tables and the spiritual agreement.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§36] [8] ...in order that the covenant of Jesus the Beloved should be sealed in our hearts in hope of his faith.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author asserts that the covenant now belongs to his followers (Christians) rather than the Jews, framed as a spiritual sealing within the group.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§43] This means that a man deserves to perish who has a knowledge of the way of righteousness, but turns aside into the way of darkness.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that possessing knowledge of religious 'righteousness' creates a specific liability; if those who are educated in the faith fail to follow it, they lose the right to salvation and deserve to perish.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§58] ...when we ourselves also have been made perfect as heirs of the covenant of the Lord. ... Since then he made us new by the remission of sins he made us another type, that we should have the soul of children... and let them rule the beasts of the earth, and the birds of heaven, and the fishes of the sea.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes a new status for the 'new people' (Christians) who, through the covenant, are granted the resource of authority and dominion over nature, though it is noted as a perfected state to be fully realized.

verbatim: [§82] [4] What then does he say in the Prophet? And let them eat of the goat which is offered in the fast for all their sins. Attend carefully,—and let all the priests alone eat the entrails unwashed with vinegar.

[§83] [5] Why? Because you are going to give to me gall and vinegar to drink when I am on the point of offering my flesh for my new people, therefore you alone shall eat, while the people fast and mourn in sackcloth and ashes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific ritual rule where priests are granted the exclusive right to consume parts of the sacrifice while the general population is excluded through fasting.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to the kingdom of God
verbatim: [§89] [11] ...Thus he says, those who will see me, and attain to my kingdom must lay hold of me through pain and suffering.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author defines the religious group (those who 'lay hold' of Jesus/the Church) as those who gain access to the kingdom of God, conditioned on suffering.

verbatim: [§98] ...But moreover the circumcision in which they trusted has been abolished. For he declared that circumcision was not of the flesh, but they erred because an evil angel was misleading them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author argues that the Jewish group (defined by circumcision in which they 'trusted') has lost the spiritual and legal validity of that physical resource/practice.

verbatim: [§97] [7] And for this reason the things which were thus done are plain to us, but obscure to them, because they did not hear the Lord’s voice.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author distinguishes between the in-group ('us') and the out-group ('them'/Israel) based on the availability of the resource of 'plain' understanding of the scriptures.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to baptism for remission of sins
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§121] [122] Concerning the water it has been written with regard to Israel that they will not receive the baptism that brings the remission of sins, but will build for themselves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author asserts that the contemporary Jewish community ('Israel') is excluded from the spiritual resource of baptismal remission as a consequence of their perceived theological errors.

extracted_rules.rule: Prohibition of consorting with unclean women
extracted_rules.resource: Right to social and physical consorting
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§108] [§116] nor shalt thou cleave to women who are unclean and who commit lawlessness in their mouths. For this animal gives birth with its mouth.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author interprets Mosaic law to forbid men from 'cleaving' to or consorting with a specific category of women ('unclean'), thereby restricting the group's social integration and access to standard communal relationships.

extracted_rules.rule: Exclusion of sinners from the council
extracted_rules.resource: Right to sit in the counsel of the righteous
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§121] [§128] Therefore the wicked shall not rise up in judgment, nor sinners in the counsel of the righteous, for the Lord knoweth the way of the righteous, and the way of the ungodly shall perish.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Based on a prophetic interpretation, the author establishes that those categorized as 'sinners' are barred from the resource of participating in the assembly or 'counsel' of the righteous.

verbatim: [§132] [11] He means to say that we go down into the water full of sins and foulness, and we come up bearing the fruit of fear in our hearts, and having hope on Jesus in the Spirit.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes the ritual of baptism as the mechanism by which the religious in-group ('we') gains purification ('come up... bearing fruit') from spiritual 'foulness'.

verbatim: [§150] [5] And Jacob crossed his hands, and placed his right hand on the head of Ephraim, the second and younger son, and blessed him... And Jacob said to Joseph... the greater shall serve the less, and this one shall indeed be blessed.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites the biblical precedent of Jacob blessing Ephraim over Manasseh to establish a rule where the 'lesser' (younger) receives the primary blessing/inheritance usually reserved for the elder.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be the covenant heir
verbatim: [§152] [7] What then does he say to Abraham, when he alone was faithful, and it was counted him for righteousness? Behold I have made thee, Abraham, the father of the Gentiles who believe in God in uncircumcision.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author uses the example of Abraham to establish that Gentiles (the uncircumcised) are now considered the legitimate heirs of the covenant through faith.

verbatim: [§132] [11] He means that whosoever hears and believes these things spoken shall live for ever.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies faith and hearing the word as the criteria for gaining the resource of eternal life.

verbatim: [§160] The Prophet therefore says, I the Lord thy God did call thee in righteousness, and I will hold thy hands, and I will give thee strength, and I have given thee for a covenant of the people, for a light to the Gentiles, to open the eyes of the blind, and to bring forth from their fetters those that are bound.
[§161] Again the Prophet says, Lo, I have made thee a light for the Gentiles, to be for salvation unto the ends of the earth, thus saith the Lord the God who did redeem thee.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies the current Christian community as those redeemed by the fulfillment of these prophecies, extending the resource of 'salvation' and 'light' to the group of Gentiles.

verbatim: [§171] Furthermore he says to them, Your new moons and the sabbaths I cannot away with. Do you see what he means? The present sabbaths are not acceptable to me, but that which I have made, in which I will give rest to all things and make the beginning of an eighth day, that is the beginning of another world.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that the traditional 'present sabbaths' observed by the Jewish people are no longer acceptable to God, meaning the group no longer has the resource of divine favor through that specific ritual.

verbatim: [§172] Wherefore we also celebrate with gladness the eighth day in which Jesus also rose from the dead, and was made manifest, and ascended into Heaven.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author establishes a new rule for the Christian group ('we'), granting them the resource of a specific holy day (the eighth day/Sunday) in contrast to the rejected seventh-day Sabbath.

verbatim: [§178] Again, it was made manifest that the city and the temple and the people of Israel were to be delivered up. For the Scripture says, And it shall come to pass in the last days that the Lord shall deliver the sheep of his pasture, and the sheep-fold, and their tower to destruction.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author notes the destruction of the physical temple as a divine decree against the 'people of Israel', removing their access to a physical holy site.

verbatim: [§181] When we received the remission of sins, and put our hope on the Name, we became new, being created again from the beginning; wherefore God truly dwells in us, in the habitation which we are.
[§183] This is a spiritual temple being built for the Lord.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author defines a new rule for believers where the 'resource' of God's presence (the temple) is shifted from a building to the group of believers themselves.

verbatim: [§195] Thou shalt not withhold thy hand from thy son or from thy daughter, but shalt teach them the fear of God from their youth up.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule for parents to provide religious instruction to their children, framing the 'fear of God' as a resource/knowledge they must receive.

verbatim: [§197] Thou shalt obey thy masters as a type of God in modesty and fear
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly demands obedience from the group defined by the absence of freedom (slaves/servants) toward their masters, conceptualizing masters as divine representatives.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from verbal abuse or bitterness
verbatim: [§197] thou shalt not command in bitterness thy slave or handmaid who hope on the same God, lest they cease to fear the God who is over you both
extracted_rules.reasoning: While in a subordinate position, the slave is granted a degree of protection from 'bitter' treatment based on shared religious status.

verbatim: [§198] Thou shalt share all things with thy neighbour and shall not say that they are thy own property; for if you are sharers in that which is incorruptible, how much more in that which is corruptible?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes a rule of communal sharing that reduces the wealthy's right to exclusive ownership of material resources.

verbatim: [§203] ...advocates of the rich, unjust judges of the poor, altogether sinful.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies the lack of impartial justice for the poor ('unjust judges') as a hallmark of the 'Way of the Black One', implying a societal rule or common practice that favors the wealthy.

verbatim: [§208] I beseech those who are in high positions, if you will receive any counsel of my goodwill, have among yourselves those to whom you may do good; fail not.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author explicitly directs a rule toward those in high social/political positions, obligating them to provide aid to others.
